questions.question: What is the first step when creating a new skill?
options: Edit the settings.json file
Create a directory under .claude/skills/ with a descriptive name
Install a skill plugin from npm
Add the skill name to CLAUDE.md
questions.explanation: The first step is to create a directory under .claude/skills/ with a descriptive kebab-case name. Then you create a SKILL.md file inside it.

questions.question: What file contains the instructions for a skill?
options: skill.json
config.yaml
SKILL.md
instructions.txt
questions.explanation: Skills are defined in SKILL.md files within their respective directories under .claude/skills/.

questions.question: Which of the following should a SKILL.md file include?
options: Only the skill name and a one-line description
A list of npm packages to install
Trigger conditions, tools to use, step-by-step process, and examples
The complete source code of the project
questions.explanation: A well-structured SKILL.md includes when to trigger, what tools to use, the step-by-step process, output format, and examples of correct usage.

questions.question: How do you test a newly created skill?
options: Run a special test command: claude-test skill
Invoke the skill in a Claude Code session and verify the output matches expectations
Submit it to the Claude Skills marketplace for validation
Skills are automatically tested when SKILL.md is saved
questions.explanation: Test a skill by invoking it in a Claude Code session, checking if the output matches expectations, identifying gaps, and iterating on the SKILL.md instructions.

questions.question: What happens when a SKILL.md file contains vague instructions like 'be careful with the code'?
options: Claude interprets it correctly using common sense
Claude produces inconsistent results because the instruction is ambiguous
Claude ignores vague instructions entirely
Claude asks the user for clarification every time
questions.explanation: Vague instructions produce inconsistent results. Claude follows skill instructions literally, so specific instructions like 'run npm run lint before committing' produce reliable, repeatable behavior.
